utterances: You're listening to SCIENCE FRIDAY.

I guess, Mayim, sort of Sheldon's girlfriend?

Well, I was his friend who was a girl, who was not his girlfriend, for a while, and we now have a relationship agreement.
So, you know, depending on which side of the Shamy you fall on, yes or no.
(SOUNDBITE OF LAUGHTER)

No, no, the industry is far more fickle and superficial than that.

No, no, there's also no choice in my profession.

Interesting that you as a female scientist are playing on an even field with Sheldon Cooper, right?
Yeah, well, I mean, they - I think that the most disturbing thing about their relationship, at least to Sheldon, is that she's not a physicist.

But he has a tremendous respect and affection for her mental capacities and her, you know, her general intellect, which I also think, you know, when challenged by feminists, I actually think that's a beautiful aspect of how they're portraying these characters.
It's never, ever been brought up at all, really, what Amy looks like or is he attracted to her.
He's attracted to her because she's brilliant, and that's enough for him despite the fact that she's not a physicist, which I think is very sweet.
It is interesting.

I assume you're asking me, you know, as a neuroscientist.

You know, there's a tremendous amount of genetic propensity not necessarily for what TV shows you like but for literally how you view the world, how you react to things, how things touch you and how things move you.

How do you...

On my side, obviously, I have a strong creative side, but also a really, really analytical, you know, philosophical side.
